Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered throughout the course. I work part-time to help cover my expenses, so I had to squeeze studying into whatever gaps I could find. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to batch my study sessions around watching lecture videos and making diagrams, usually late at night or early mornings when I had quiet time. Some weeks I'd put in serious hours, other weeks life just got in the way.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what shifts I had at work. When deadlines approached, I'd go into overdrive mode and study intensely for several days straight. During calmer weeks, I might not touch the course materials as much because I was catching up on sleep or handling other responsibilities. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or knowing I had fallen behind that pushed me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a new assignment or discussion, and that would prompt me to check in. I also logged in when I knew I had a chunk of free time and wanted to download videos to watch later when I might not have reliable internet.
Q4: For a while, I got into a rhythm of logging in every Sunday evening to plan out my week and see what was coming up. That became almost automatic for about a month. But then my work schedule changed and I lost that consistency. The mindfulness exercises actually helped me be more intentional about checking in, even if just briefly.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by seeing things visually explained. I'd pause, rewind, and really study the diagrams and examples they showed. The discussion forums were also helpful when classmates would share their own visual notes or explain concepts differently than the professor. Those peer explanations sometimes clicked better for me.
Q6: I often skipped the long reading PDFs because they felt overwhelming and I couldn't engage with walls of text the same way I could with videos. I also didn't participate much in discussions beyond reading them because typing out responses took time I didn't always have. The quizzes I sometimes delayed until the last possible moment because my anxiety would spike thinking about them.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate relevance to what I needed to understand or when it would directly help my grade. If something felt like busywork or didn't connect to the bigger picture, I'd deprioritize it. Visual content that I could actually learn from quickly always felt more valuable than lengthy text-based activities.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford time-wise. With required activities, there was external pressure that helped me push through my anxiety and just get them done. Optional stuff, even if interesting, often got pushed aside because I had to triage my limited time and energy toward what would actually impact my grade.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture videos and watch them on my phone during bus rides to work or during breaks. I also made handwritten visual notes and diagrams that I'd study from repeatedly. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos that explained the same concepts differently, which really helped solidify my understanding.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with the material but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd print out slides and annotate them heavily while rewatching downloaded videos. Or when I'd study with a classmate in person, working through problems together on a whiteboard. That kind of active learning doesn't leave any digital trace.
Q11: Yeah, this was actually my main strategy for a lot of content. I'd access a video once, download it, and then watch it multiple times offline over the following days. Same with PDFs that I'd print out when I could afford to. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so downloading and studying offline just made more sense.
Q12: Mid-semester was really tough when my work increased my hours and I had assignments piling up across multiple courses. My anxiety got pretty bad during that period, and even though I wanted to engage, I felt paralyzed sometimes. The mindfulness intervention actually came at a good time and helped me manage that anxiety enough to keep pushing forward.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by everything I had to do, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between studying and other necessities like working or sleeping. Fatigue made it hard to engage meaningfully even when I did have time.
Q14: There were times I'd open a video and let it play while my mind was elsewhere, worrying about bills or my work schedule. I'd technically be 'watching' but not absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them just to feel like I was doing something productive when I was too anxious to focus properly.
Q15: I postponed assignments a lot, even important ones, because my anxiety would spike whenever I thought about starting them. The fear of not doing well enough would paralyze me, so I'd avoid it until the deadline forced my hand. I knew it was counterproductive, but in the moment, avoidance felt like the only way to manage the overwhelming feelings.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I might have drifted completely. They created enough pressure to override my anxiety and force me into action. I'd often do most of my LMS activity in the 24-48 hours before something was due, which wasn't healthy but it's how I survived the semester.
Q17: When I got positive feedback on assignments, it genuinely boosted my confidence and made me more willing to engage with the next one. But when feedback was delayed or unclear, I'd feel uncertain about whether I was on the right track, which fed my anxiety. Quick, encouraging feedback helped me stay motivated despite my struggles.
Q18: The course structure with its multiple online courses and flexible deadlines was both a blessing and a curse. It gave me flexibility to work around my schedule, but it also meant I could easily fall behind without immediate consequences. Having clearer weekly milestones might have helped me stay more consistent instead of cramming everything.
Q19: The visual elements in the course materials definitely made engagement easier for me. But the sheer volume of content across five online courses made it hard to keep up. When materials were broken into smaller chunks, I could engage better. Long, dense modules were harder to tackle, especially when my anxiety was high.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's anxious and keeps checking the LMS, reading and re-reading things, but then gets paralyzed when it comes to actually submitting work. The fear of not being good enough can make you engage with materials obsessively while still struggling to produce finished assignments. It's a frustrating cycle.
Q21: That actually describes my situation somewhat. My assignment completion rate doesn't look great, and my LMS activity was low, but I still passed because I studied effectively offline. I downloaded materials, made my own visual notes, and studied in ways that worked for my learning style. The LMS activity doesn't capture that effort.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone logged in and what they clicked on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone was deeply focused or just mindlessly scrolling. They also miss all the offline studying, the thinking that happens away from the screen, and the emotional state behind the behavior.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, and connecting new information to what I already know. For me, true engagement often happens when I'm drawing diagrams or explaining concepts to myself out loud, not necessarily when I'm logged into a system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was battling anxiety just to open my assignments, or how the mindfulness exercises helped me actually complete the semester. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying on downloaded materials during bus rides, or how much peer support from classmates kept me going. My resilience and effort don't show up in click data.
